title: Incorrectly Calculating the Height of a Binary Tree

statement.markdown: Given a binary tree, calculate the height of the tree. The height of a binary tree is the number of edges on the longest path from the root node down to the farthest leaf node.
statement.inputFormat: Example input (matching the stdin format that main wrapper expects)
statement.notes: Any other rules, e.g. time/space expectations

constraints.description: Number of nodes in the binary tree

examples.explanation: The height of the tree is 3 because the path from the root node to the farthest leaf is 1-2-4
The height of the tree is 4 because the path from the root node to the farthest leaf is 1-2-3-6

hints.text: Use level order traversal or recursive DFS to calculate the height of the tree

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: static int height(TreeNode* root) { // TODO: implement } };
starterCode.java: public class Solution { public static int height(TreeNode root) { // TODO: implement } }
starterCode.python: class Solution: @staticmethod def height(root: TreeNode) -> int: # TODO: implement pass

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: static int height(TreeNode* root) { if (root == nullptr) return 0; return 1 + height(root->left) + height(root->right); } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public static int height(TreeNode root) { if (root == null) return 0; return 1 + height(root.left) + height(root.right); } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: @staticmethod def height(root: TreeNode) -> int: if not root: return 0 return 1 + height(root.left) + height(root.right)
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function height(root) { if (root == null) return 0; return 1 + height(root.left) + height(root.right); }
